sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on the Australian Macadamia Society, Queensland and Western Australian government agriculture sources, horticultural research, and the WANATCA and Rare Fruit Council of Australia archives.

core.intro: At heart a macadamia is grown the same way anywhere in Australia: a warm, frost-free, sheltered spot, deep free-draining soil, a grafted named tree (ideally two, for cross-pollination), and patience while it grows. It is the only Australian native plant grown as a major commercial food crop, and every macadamia in the world traces back to a few wild trees in the rainforests of southeast Queensland and northern New South Wales. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Two species, one nut
sections.body: There are two edible macadamia species, and most trees you can buy are one of them or a hybrid of the two. Macadamia integrifolia, the smooth-shell macadamia, comes from southeast Queensland, usually carries its leaves in whorls of three and has creamy white flowers. Macadamia tetraphylla, the rough-shell macadamia, comes from northern New South Wales, usually carries its leaves in fours, has pink flowers, and a higher-sugar kernel that browns and can scorch sooner when roasted. Popular garden varieties such as Beaumont are integrifolia by tetraphylla hybrids that borrow useful traits from both. Only these two species and their hybrids give the sweet edible nut: some wild macadamia relatives have bitter, mildly toxic kernels, so buy a named edible cultivar rather than an unidentified seedling.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Buy a grafted, named cultivar rather than a seedling, because it fruits years sooner and grows true to type. For a backyard, the hybrid Beaumont is the popular all-rounder, an easy, heavy bearer with showy pink flowers and nuts that are simple to dehusk and crack. The modern Australian orchard varieties are the patented A series bred by Henry Bell at Hidden Valley Plantations near Beerwah, Queensland, from 1975 onward: A4 is precocious and high-yielding, A16 is a naturally compact, semi-dwarf tree that suits a smaller garden, and A38, A203 and Daddow are other proven Australian selections. The older Hawaiian selections, sold by their breeding-station numbers such as 246 (Keauhou), 344, 816 and 849, are still grown but were generally out-yielded by the local Australian varieties in Australian trials. For a pot or a courtyard, look for a genuine dwarf such as A16. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

sections.heading: Pollination: plant more than one
sections.body: Macadamias will set some nuts on their own, but most cultivars are partly or wholly self-incompatible and crop far better with a second, different variety flowering nearby, because cross-pollination lifts both the number of nuts and the size of the kernel. Bees do the work: honeybees and native stingless bees are the main pollinators, and a flower raceme needs many bee visits to set a full crop. If you have room, plant two or three different cultivars that flower at the same time and keep them reasonably close together; documented good partners include A29 with 849 or 741. A single tree will still give you nuts, but a compatible neighbour will give you noticeably more.

sections.heading: Planting, soil and shelter
sections.body: Give a macadamia full sun and a deep, free-draining, slightly acidic soil. It dislikes waterlogging, which invites root rot, so avoid any spot where water pools and plant on a low mound if your soil is heavy. The thing growers most often underrate is shelter: macadamia wood is brittle and limbs snap in strong wind, and losing a major limb throws the tree into leafy regrowth at the expense of nuts, so choose a protected position or plant a windbreak. A seedling can reach 15 to 20 metres over decades, but grafted garden varieties stay much smaller and take pruning well; a semi-dwarf such as A16 sits around three to four metres and can spend its early years in a large, deep pot (see our guide on when to plant). Its only nut-tree cousin in our listings is the pecan, a cold-climate, deciduous tree that wants nearly the opposite conditions.

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Macadamias are fairly drought-hardy once established, helped by dense surface roots and tough leaves, but they reward water at the key stages: flowering, nut set, and especially the summer oil-filling period, when moisture stress makes a tree shed nuts. Water young trees through their first few summers and keep any bearing tree watered while the nuts are filling. Feed lightly and often rather than heavily, because too much nitrogen drives leafy growth at the expense of flowering, so split the nitrogen and potassium across the year; watch for boron, which is often low in Australian soils and, sprayed over the flowers, reduces premature nut drop. On the much-debated question of phosphorus: macadamia is in the protea family and its cluster roots are sensitive to a large excess of phosphorus (old vegetable ground loaded with superphosphate can harm a young tree), so a low-phosphorus fertiliser blended for Australian native plants is a safe default for a backyard tree, fed under the canopy in autumn and spring over a thick mulch of compost or old husks. But it is a myth that a macadamia must never see phosphorus: commercial orchards do feed it, guided by soil tests, and the tree tolerates levels that would harm an ornamental banksia. Aim for a slightly acidic soil (about pH 5.5 to 6.5) and let a soil or leaf test settle any doubt.

sections.heading: Harvest and drying the nut
sections.body: Macadamias tell you when they are ready by dropping to the ground: the nuts mature over about five to six months and fall once the kernel has filled with oil, so the job is to gather them, not pick them. Do not pull green nuts off the tree, because immature nuts are low in oil and will not ripen once off the branch. Rake and collect the fallen nuts at least weekly, then remove the fibrous husk straight away, within a day of harvest, because a damp husk heats up and taints the kernel. Spread the husked nuts one layer deep in a ventilated tray out of the sun and dry them slowly for two to three weeks, then finish them somewhere warm and dry until the kernels rattle loose and turn crisp. Only then crack them: the shell is famously hard and a proper macadamia cracker earns its keep.

sections.heading: Eating, storing and safety
sections.body: Unlike olives, macadamias need no curing: they are delicious raw from a well-dried nut, or lightly roasted (rough-shell and hybrid kernels are higher in sugar and brown faster, so roast them gently). Because the kernel is around three-quarters oil, it turns rancid if left warm, so store cracked nuts in an airtight container in the fridge or freezer. Two safety notes are worth knowing: only Macadamia integrifolia, Macadamia tetraphylla and their hybrids are edible, while some wild macadamia relatives carry bitter, cyanide-bearing kernels; and macadamia nuts are toxic to dogs even though they are perfectly safe for people, so keep harvested and fallen nuts away from pets.

sections.heading: An Australian native worth growing
sections.body: The macadamia is a genuine piece of Australian natural heritage. It is the only native plant grown as a major commercial food crop, and the entire world industry descends from a handful of wild trees. Those wild stands are now under pressure, with much of the original rainforest habitat cleared and several wild macadamia species listed as threatened, including the critically endangered Macadamia jansenii. Planting a named garden tree has nothing to do with that decline, but it is a nice reason to grow one, and to buy nursery-propagated stock rather than anything taken from the bush.

faqs.q: How long before a macadamia tree produces nuts?
faqs.a: A grafted, named cultivar usually starts bearing within about three to five years and builds to full production over roughly ten to fifteen. A seedling takes far longer, often seven to twelve years, and the nuts are unpredictable, which is why named, grafted stock is worth paying for.

faqs.q: Can I grow a macadamia in a pot or a small garden?
faqs.a: Yes, while it is young. A dwarf or semi-dwarf such as A16 will grow happily for several years in a large, deep, free-draining container on a sunny, sheltered patio. Keep it watered and fed because pots dry out and lose nutrients fast, prune it to a single leader, and accept a lighter crop than a tree in the ground; a full-size variety eventually wants open soil to crop well.

faqs.q: Are macadamia nuts poisonous to dogs?
faqs.a: Yes. Macadamia nuts are toxic to dogs and can cause weakness, tremors and vomiting, even though the same nuts are completely safe for people. Keep your harvest, and any nuts that fall under the tree, out of a dog's reach.

faqs.q: Why is my macadamia healthy but not setting nuts?
faqs.a: The usual reasons are youth (even grafted trees take a few years), having only one tree so cross-pollination is poor, too much shade, or heavy feeding (especially too much nitrogen) pushing leaves instead of flowers; wind that strips flowers or breaks limbs can also cost a crop. Give it full sun, a compatible second variety, and a lighter hand with high-nitrogen fertiliser.

WA.intro: Growing macadamias in Western Australia. The macadamia is an east-coast native, but the warm, dry, Mediterranean southwest grows it well with irrigation, and individual WA trees have cropped for the best part of a century.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The southwest of WA has a Mediterranean climate, with hot dry summers and mild wet winters, much like the macadamia districts of California and quite unlike the humid east coast the tree comes from. Macadamias take to it: they want a frost-free site and grow best between about 25 and 35 degrees. The catch is water. WA's rain falls in winter, while the tree needs water in summer for flowering and nut filling, so irrigation is essential rather than optional, and fertigation (feeding through the irrigation lines) is the local norm.

sections.heading: Where macadamias grow in WA
sections.body: Macadamias will grow and crop anywhere in the southwest that escapes hard frosts, a belt running roughly from Northampton in the north down to Albany in the south. Small plantings dot the Margaret River and Busselton area, around Quindalup and Cowaramup, and the Perth hills districts such as Chittering. The crop has a long backyard history here too: a macadamia planted at Kings Park in the 1920s still fruits each year, as do trees of both species planted at the University of Western Australia in the 1930s.

sections.heading: Harvest in WA
sections.body: WA's plantings are small and there is little published on local timing, but expect the usual pattern: the nuts ripen and fall in the cooler months, roughly autumn into winter. Gather them off the ground promptly, dehusk within a day, and dry them as for any macadamia (see the core notes below).

sections.heading: Water and feeding in WA
sections.body: Because the dry summer falls exactly when the tree is flowering and filling nuts, deep, regular summer irrigation makes the difference between a light crop and a good one. WA's coastal soils are deep, infertile sands and its hill soils are old and weathered, both naturally low in phosphorus, so a WA tree, unlike one on the rich eastern soils, usually does need some phosphorus along with its nitrogen, potassium and a heavy mulch. Windbreaks earn their keep on WA's windy sites.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from interstate must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List and usually treated and certified before entry, and many eastern nurseries will not post live trees here at all. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA, which is why a smaller range shows here than for the eastern states. There is no macadamia-specific ban; it is the general plant-import rule.

faqs.q: Which macadamia variety suits Western Australia?
faqs.a: Any of the proven hybrids and Australian selections will grow in the southwest with irrigation. The hybrid Beaumont is a dependable backyard choice, and the compact A16 suits a smaller garden or a large pot. Choose a grafted, named tree and plant a second, different variety nearby for better pollination.

faqs.q: Can I grow a macadamia in Perth's sandy soil?
faqs.a: Yes. Macadamias cope with free-draining Perth sand as long as you improve it with plenty of compost and keep a thick mulch over the surface roots, which is where the tree feeds. Water deeply through summer because sand dries fast, and feed little and often because nutrients leach quickly. A long-standing seedling in suburban Perth sand has cropped well on little more than ordinary garden watering.

QLD.intro: Growing macadamias in Queensland. Queensland is the macadamia's original home and now the country's biggest producer, so the tree is about as at-home as a nut tree gets here.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Southeast Queensland's warm, humid, frost-free subtropics are the macadamia's native climate, so the tree needs little persuading. It grows best between about 25 and 35 degrees, dislikes hard frost while young, and wants its water through the spring and summer flowering and nut-fill period. Coastal and near-coastal districts from the New South Wales border up through the Sunshine Coast and Wide Bay suit it well.

sections.heading: Where macadamias grow in QLD
sections.body: Bundaberg is now Australia's single largest macadamia region, having overtaken the long-standing New South Wales heartland in 2016 as cane land switched to nut orchards, and it grows well over half the national crop. The older districts around Gympie, the Glasshouse Mountains and the Sunshine Coast hinterland remain important, and there are newer plantings further north around Mackay and Emerald. Nearby Mount Bauple is remembered as the ancestral home of the cultivated macadamia.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland flowers a little earlier than the south, often from around August, so its harvest tends to open and close a few weeks ahead of the cooler districts. The mature nuts fall through autumn and winter, broadly March to September, and are gathered from the ground rather than picked.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: The main insect pest is the macadamia nut borer, a native moth whose larvae bore into the nut and cause it to drop; it occurs across Queensland's macadamia districts. Fruit-spotting bug damages nuts wherever orchards sit near native bush, and the macadamia seed weevil, first found on the Atherton Tablelands, is an emerging concern in the north. Husk spot, a fungal disease that drops nuts, is managed largely by stripping the old hanging husks from the tree. The macadamia felted coccid, a tiny native scale, is usually kept in check here by its natural enemies, despite being a notorious invader overseas. Phytophthora root rot strikes trees on poorly drained ground, so drainage is the first defence.

faqs.q: Which macadamia varieties are best in Queensland?
faqs.a: For a home tree the hybrid Beaumont is hard to beat, while gardeners who want a smaller tree often choose the semi-dwarf A16. If you want the modern commercial types, A4 and 816 are proven Queensland performers. Whichever you pick, plant a second, different variety for cross-pollination.

faqs.q: Do macadamias get fruit fly in Queensland?
faqs.a: No. The macadamia's hard, woody shell means it is not a host of Queensland fruit fly, so the traps and baits that citrus and stone fruit need are not part of growing macadamias. The pests to watch instead are the nut borer and, near bushland, the fruit-spotting bug.

NSW.intro: Growing macadamias in New South Wales. The modern macadamia industry was born on the New South Wales north coast, and the rough-shell species is native to the region, so it is a natural backyard tree across the warmer parts of the state.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The warm, high-rainfall north coast is prime macadamia country and the native home of the rough-shell Macadamia tetraphylla. The tree wants a frost-free or near-frost-free site: it crops happily on the humid coast and the lower ranges, while the cold tablelands and the dry western slopes are marginal. Generous rainfall, often 1,800 millimetres or more on the coast, suits it, though that same humidity raises disease pressure.

sections.heading: Where macadamias grow in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers around Lismore is the heartland, where the first commercial orchard was planted in the 1880s and the Rous Mill plantings are still bearing well over a century later. Alstonville and Dunoon on the plateau, the Clarence Valley, and the mid north coast strip from the Nambucca Valley up to Woolgoolga all carry orchards.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the New South Wales coast the trees flower in spring, fill their nuts with oil over summer, and drop them through autumn and winter, with the heaviest fall often around late May and June and harvest running for several months. The cooler, wetter coast tends to ripen a little later than the Queensland crop. Gather the nuts off the ground promptly and dehusk them within a day.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Humidity is the main challenge. Husk spot, a fungal disease that causes premature nut drop, presses hardest in the wet Northern Rivers and is managed by removing the old husks that harbour it. The macadamia nut borer and fruit-spotting bug are both active, and the macadamia seed weevil has become established in the region. The shell's hardness does at least mean the nut is not a fruit fly host. The native macadamia felted coccid is usually held in check by natural enemies unless broad-spectrum sprays disrupt them.

faqs.q: Which macadamia variety should I plant on the NSW coast?
faqs.a: Both species grow here, and the local rough-shell Macadamia tetraphylla and its hybrids, such as Beaumont, are well suited to the warm, humid climate. For a smaller garden the semi-dwarf A16 is a good pick. Plant two different cultivars for cross-pollination.

faqs.q: How do I stop rats and birds taking my macadamias?
faqs.a: Rats are the most damaging pest of a backyard macadamia, gnawing nuts both in the tree and on the ground, and cockatoos and parrots take their share too. Collect fallen nuts often so they are not left as bait, keep mulch and rubbish away from the trunk, and harvest promptly rather than leaving a ripe crop hanging.

VIC.intro: Growing macadamias in Victoria. Victoria is not macadamia country, but in the warmest, most frost-free corners of the state a sheltered tree can be grown as a backyard curiosity.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Macadamias are subtropical and frost-tender when young, so Victoria's cold winters and frosts are the real limit, and there is no commercial macadamia industry in the state. In the mildest, most frost-free pockets, such as the milder coastal districts including Gippsland and the warmest, most sheltered suburbs of Melbourne, a tree can grow and may set a light crop in good years. Expect slow growth, late and modest crops, and a real risk of losing an unprotected young tree to a hard frost.

sections.heading: Getting a young tree through winter
sections.body: The hardest part in Victoria is the first few winters, while the tree is most frost-tender. Plant against a warm, north-facing wall in the sunniest, most sheltered spot, mulch well, and protect young trees with frost cloth on cold nights; many gardeners grow a dwarf macadamia in a large pot so it can be moved under cover for winter. An established tree shrugs off light frost far better than a youngster.

sections.heading: Harvest and pests in VIC
sections.body: If a Victorian tree does crop, the cool climate pushes the harvest later, into winter and spring. On the upside, the cool, drier-summer climate gives little of the husk-spot and insect pressure that the warm, humid north contends with, so here the limiting factor is the climate itself, not pests; rats and cockatoos will still help themselves to any nuts that form.

faqs.q: Will a macadamia actually produce nuts in Melbourne?
faqs.a: It can, but do not count on it. In a warm, frost-free, sheltered Melbourne garden a macadamia may set a light crop in good years, though the cool season means slow growth and unreliable cropping. Many people here grow one as much for its handsome evergreen foliage and pink-flowered hybrids as for the nuts.

faqs.q: Which macadamia is best for a cool Victorian garden?
faqs.a: Choose the more cold-tolerant rough-shell Macadamia tetraphylla, or a hybrid such as Beaumont, over the tender smooth-shell types, and ideally a dwarf or semi-dwarf you can grow in a pot and shelter over winter. A grafted, named tree also fruits sooner, which matters where the season is short.
